sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=17).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 115, level 2.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'SPACE': 'Changes board state (timer/HUD) but does NOT complete level (VERIFIED steps 32-113)', 'MOUSE': 'Click dot to select (white border), click another dot to swap colors (VERIFIED step 40); cross-rect dot clicks swap colors between red and green rects (VERIFIED step 75)', 'ACTION7': 'Unknown action - gives error when called (VERIFIED step 104)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'top_panel': '7 colored squares at row 1: [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M] - likely target pattern or palette', 'red_rect': 'Red-bordered container at rows 18-25 with 3 paintable dots at cols 21,27,39', 'green_rect': 'Green-bordered container at rows 30-41 with 4 paintable dots at cols 21,27,33,39', 'T_shape': 'Light green (N) connector between red and green rects', 'bottom_squares': '7 gray squares at row 58 - NOT interactive (VERIFIED step 103)'}
- HUD map: {'timer_bar': 'row 53, charcoal bar that changes with SPACE', 'bottom_squares': 'rows 58, gray squares, not interactive'}
- Win pattern: {'unknown': 'SPACE does not complete level even with correct-looking arrangements. All traversal orders tried and failed.'}
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Paint 4 dots with bottom colors, SPACE to submit', 'level_2': 'In progress - 113 actions, 6+ arrangements tried, SPACE never completes'}
- Cross-level notes: {'level_2_mechanic': 'Swapping colors between dots via click-select then click-swap. Cross-rect swapping works.'}
Current level:
- World model: Current: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]. Tried Green L→R + Red R→L traversal matching top row [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]. SPACE still fails.
- Goal model: Find correct arrangement or discover mechanic beyond arranging dots. All simple traversal orders exhausted.
- Recent findings: ['ACTION7 gives error', 'Bottom gray squares not interactive', '6 different dot arrangements + SPACE all failed', 'Only SPACE and MOUSE are functional actions']
- Open questions: ["Is there a correct arrangement I haven't tried?", 'Does the goal involve something other than matching the top row?', "Could the T-shape or borders be interactive in a way I haven't tried?", 'Could the white corner pieces be clickable?']
- Plan: Try clicking on white corner pieces of red rect, or try completely different non-arrangement approach
- Strategy log: ['Tried Red L→R + Green L→R: failed', 'Tried Green L→R + Red L→R: failed', 'Tried column-major reading: failed', 'Tried reversed orders: failed', 'Tried T-shape path traversal (Green L→R, Red R→L): failed', 'All 6 arrangement hypotheses exhausted - need fundamentally different approach']
- Failed probes: ['SPACE at step 32 (before all dots painted)', 'ACTION7 (error: unknown action)', 'MOUSE(22,34) click c dot (no effect)', 'MOUSE(8,40) click top N (no effect)', 'SPACE x3 at steps 35-37 (no color shift, no submit)', 'MOUSE(8,10) click top O while dot selected (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,p,R], Green=[b,N,Y,M] (step 53)', 'SPACE after Green=[O,p,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'SPACE after Green=[p,O,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'MOUSE(38,21) then MOUSE(38,27) palette clicks + SPACE', 'MOUSE(58,10) bottom square click (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,R,Y], Green=[p,b,N,M] (column-major)', 'SPACE after Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b] (T-shape path)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 99 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The white corner click at (17,17) didn't produce a board change (I need to check). Let me look at the result. The action was executed but I don't see the result details. Let me check if the board changed.
Now I have the exact dot positions from the segmentation:
- Top row squares (20 pixels each): centers at (3,10), (3,17), (3,24), (3,31), (3,38), (3,45), (3,52)
- Red rect dots (16 pixels each): centers at (22,22), (22,28), (22,40) - colors M, Y, N
- Green rect dots (16 pixels each): centers at (36,22), (36,28), (36,34), (36,40) - colors O, p, R, b
I've tried many arrangements. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. I haven't tried ALL permutations of the 7 colors across 7 positions. There are 7! = 5040 permutations, but I've only tried a handful.
2. But trying random permutations is not efficient.
Let me reconsider the problem. The top row has 7 colors. The red rect has 3 dots and green has 4 dots. The T-shape connects them.
What if the goal is NOT to match the top row at all? What if the goal is:
- Each rect's dots should be sorted by some property
- Or each rect's dots should match the border color in some way
- Or the dots should be arranged to form a specific pattern
Wait, let me reconsider the level 1 mechanic. In level 1, the bottom row was a PALETTE - clicking on a color in the bottom row painted the selected dot. What if in level 2, the top row is also a palette, but the interaction is different?
What if:
1. You click a dot to select it
2. You click a color in the top row to PAINT that dot with that color
3. This would let you change any dot to any color
I tried "MOUSE(8,10) click top O while dot selected (no effect)" but the top row is at row 3, not row 8. Let me try clicking on the actual top row squares at row 3.
Let me try: select a dot, then click on a top row color square to paint it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if white corner click did anything
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'unknown'))

# New hypothesis: top row is a PALETTE (like level 1 bottom row)
# Click dot to select, then click top row color to PAINT that dot
# Top row centers: (3,10)=O, (3,17)=p, (3,24)=R, (3,31)=b, (3,38)=N, (3,45)=Y, (3,52)=M
# Current dots: Red=[M,Y,N] at (22,22),(22,28),(22,40); Green=[O,p,R,b] at (36,22),(36,28),(36,34),(36,40)

# Let me try: select a red dot, then click top row color to paint it
# If this works, I can paint all dots to match top row, then SPACE
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},  # select Red dot (currently M)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 3, 'col': 10},   # click top O to paint it orange
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Changes board state but does NOT complete level", "MOUSE": "Click dot to select, click another dot to swap colors; may also paint from top row palette", "ACTION7": "Unknown action - gives error"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "7 colored squares at row 3: [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M] - possibly a palette", "red_rect": "3 paintable dots at (22,22),(22,28),(22,40)", "green_rect": "4 paintable dots at (36,22),(36,28),(36,34),(36,40)", "white_corners": "4 white pieces on red rect - not interactive"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row 53"}, "win_pattern": {"unknown": "SPACE never completes level"}, "level_log": {"level_2": "115 actions, all arrangements failed"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]", "goal_model": "Testing if top row is a palette for painting dots", "recent_findings": ["White corner click had no effect"], "open_questions": ["Can top row colors paint dots?"], "current_plan": "Select dot, click top row color to test painting mechanic", "strategy_log": ["6+ arrangements failed, trying palette mechanic"], "failed_probes": ["All arrangements + SPACE", "ACTION7", "bottom squares", "white corners", "T-shape dot"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<parameter=code>
# Check if white corner click did anything
